title: Hallucination in Summarization
concept: A major risk: the model generates plausible-sounding facts that were not in the original document, requiring strict validation.

exercises.prompt: What does 'hallucination' mean when we talk about AI summarization?

options.text: When the AI summary is too short.
When the AI invents facts not found in the original document.
When the AI translates the document correctly.

exercises.explain: Hallucinations occur when an AI generates plausible-sounding facts, numbers, or details that are completely missing from the original source text.

cards.title: Truth or Fiction?
cards.body: AI summaries can be super helpful, but they have a sneaky habit. Sometimes, they confidently tell lies that sound 100% true! Let's learn how to spot these imaginary facts and keep our summaries accurate.

cards.title: What is Hallucination?
cards.body: In AI summarization, a hallucination happens when the model generates a summary that includes facts, events, or numbers that were never in the original text. The output might sound professional and highly convincing, but it is actually incorrect or unverified.

cards.title: The Creative Waiter
cards.body: Imagine a helpful assistant reading a restaurant menu. The menu only lists pizza and pasta. When you ask for a summary, the assistant says: 'They serve amazing tacos!' Tacos might be delicious, but they weren't on the menu. That is exactly what a hallucinating AI does—it invents details out of thin air!

cards.title: Spotting the Fake News
cards.body:
Original text: 'The company launched its first electric car in 2021.'

Hallucinated summary: 'The company has been leading the electric car market since 2015, launching several models.'

Why it's a hallucination: The year 2015 and the mention of 'several models' were completely made up by the AI.

cards.title: The Fluent Liar
cards.body:
Myth: If an AI summary is written in perfect grammar and sounds professional, the facts inside it must be true.
Reality: AI models are language experts, not truth finders. They can write beautiful, fluent sentences that are completely fabricated. Always verify!

cards.title: Factual vs. Hallucinated
cards.body: Let's look at how a factual summary compares to a hallucinated one based on the source text: 'A new park opened on Main Street.'

left.text: A new park is now open on Main Street.

right.text: A giant theme park with rollercoasters opened on Main Street.

exercises.prompt: Why are hallucinations in summaries so dangerous?

options.text: The text becomes too short to read.
They sound highly plausible and realistic, making them hard to spot.
The AI changes the language of the summary to French.
The summary is saved in the wrong folder.

exercises.explain: Hallucinations are risky because the AI writes them so confidently and naturally that they look completely correct at first glance.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you feed a document to an AI, it is impossible for the AI to include facts that were not in that document.
exercises.explain: AI models do not 'know' facts; they predict words. They can easily generate false facts that were not in your source document.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe when an AI invents facts:
exercises.sentence: When an AI adds realistic but fake facts to a summary, it is called a ____________.
exercises.explain: A hallucination is when the AI generates convincing but completely unsupported information that was not in the source text.

exercises.prompt: Original text: 'The space team delayed the rocket launch to next year due to technical errors.' Which summary contains a hallucination?

options.text: Summary 1: 'The space team postponed their rocket launch until next year.'
Summary 2: 'The team successfully sent their rocket to space yesterday.'
Summary 3: 'Due to technical issues, the rocket launch was delayed.'

exercises.explain: The second summary is a hallucination because the original text explicitly states the launch was postponed to next year, meaning no rockets have been sent yet.

exercises.prompt: Since AI hallucinations look and sound completely realistic, what is the best practice when using AI to summarize important documents?

options.text: Trust the summary if the AI uses a highly confident tone.
Assume the summary is perfect if there are no spelling mistakes.
Always compare the summary's facts directly against the original text.
Only check the first sentence of the summary for accuracy.

exercises.explain: AI models are highly skilled at making up details that sound totally convincing. Therefore, you must always double-check the summary against the original document, rather than relying on how confident or fluent the AI sounds.
